2564	\section{Pullback-monoidality: invertibility under a general scheme morphism}
2565	\label{sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality}
2566	
2567	The relative Picard consumer (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) re-bases its
2568	line-bundle carrier onto the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2569	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and its
2570	functorial structure maps are \emph{pullback} maps of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules: the
2571	projection pullback \(\pi_T^*\) along \(C \times_S T \to T\) and the base-change
2572	pullback along \(C \times_S T' \to C \times_S T\). For these maps to descend to the
2573	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) carrier one needs that \emph{pullback preserves
2574	\(\otimes\)-invertibility} for \emph{general} morphisms of schemes --- the
2575	projection and the base-change morphism are neither open immersions nor flat.
2576	
2577	This is a genuinely different fact from the open-immersion compatibility
2578	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and not a corollary of it. That lemma handles
2579	restriction along an open immersion, and it does so by routing through the
2580	\emph{lax right} adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is
2581	strong monoidal there \emph{only} because an open immersion's structure-sheaf ring
2582	map is a local \emph{isomorphism} (\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\)). A general scheme
2583	morphism has no such isomorphism, so the right-adjoint route is unavailable.
2584	
2585	The general case is the \emph{left} adjoint, pullback \(f^* = \) inverse image
2586	\(=(\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\). Conceptually pullback \emph{is}
2587	``extension of scalars'', and extension of scalars along any ring map \emph{is} strong
2588	monoidal --- the base-change tensorator \((M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S \cong
2589	(M \otimes_R S) \otimes_S (N \otimes_R S)\) is an isomorphism with no flatness
2590	hypothesis. So at the level of mathematics there is no obstacle: pullback commutes
2591	with \(\otimes\) for every morphism. The one genuine subtlety is purely structural to
2592	the formalisation: the project carries \(f^*\) \emph{abstractly}, as the left adjoint
2593	\((\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\), so that \((f^*).\mathrm{obj}\) has
2594	no sectionwise value to which one could literally attach the \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)
2595	tensorator. The resolution is the standard one for an abstract left adjoint: build the
2596	strong-monoidal structure on a \emph{concrete} model \(P\) of the pullback and transport
2597	it to \(f^*\) along the uniqueness of left adjoints. This dissolves the
2598	``no sectionwise formula'' worry entirely --- one never computes \(f^*\) sectionwise;
2599	one transports a concrete monoidal structure across an adjoint-uniqueness isomorphism.
2600	This is exactly the device that closed \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
2601	
2602	It is worth recording why two tempting shortcuts are not taken. First, one might hope
2603	that since pullback is \emph{some} (op)lax monoidal functor, invertible objects are
2604	preserved for free. This is \emph{false}: global sections \(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal,
2605	yet \(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not an invertible module, so a mere
2606	oplax comparison map does \emph{not} suffice --- one genuinely has to prove the tensor
2607	comparison is an \emph{isomorphism}. Second, one might route through local triviality,
2608	``invertible \(\Rightarrow\) locally \(\mathcal{O}\) \(\Rightarrow\) pulls back to
2609	locally \(\mathcal{O}\)''. This is a mirage on the present carrier: recovering
2610	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) from local triviality is the reverse bridge
2611	\(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsInvertible}\), i.e.\ exactly the
2612	dual/tensor-inverse manufacture that the invertibility-carrier pivot
2613	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}) was built to delete. Restricting to \emph{flat}
2614	structure maps does not help either: flatness governs the exactness of \(f^*\), not
2615	whether the tensor comparison is an isomorphism (it is an isomorphism for \emph{every}
2616	morphism, flat or not). The strong-monoidal pullback route is therefore primary
2617	precisely because it pulls back the existing tensor-inverse witness directly, through a
2618	comparison isomorphism valid for all morphisms, never reintroducing the deleted dual.
2619	
2620	\begin{lemma}
2621	[Pullback commutes with $\otimes$ for a general scheme morphism]
2622	  \label{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}
2623	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackTensorIso}
2624	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
2625	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
2626	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
2627	  Writing \(f^* = \mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f\) for the inverse-image functor
2628	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules, the canonical comparison
2629	  \[
2630	    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
2631	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2632	    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)
2633	  \]
2634	  is an isomorphism, natural in \(M\) and \(N\). No local-freeness, flatness, or
2635	  open-immersion hypothesis is required: it holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
2636	  and all morphisms \(f\).

2638	  % \ref{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} (pullback of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a
2639	  % morphism of ringed spaces commutes with tensor product, functorially)
2640	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2392-2400).

2652	  \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}: for a morphism of ringed spaces
2653	  \(f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)\) and \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules

2658	\begin{proof}
2659	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
2660	  Mathematically, pullback is extension of scalars and extension of scalars is strong
2661	  monoidal, so the comparison is an isomorphism for every \(f\). The only work is to
2662	  carry this through the formalisation's \emph{abstract} pullback \(f^* =
2663	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\), which has no sectionwise value. We
2664	  do not compute \(f^*\) sectionwise; instead we equip a \emph{concrete} model of the
2665	  pullback with a strong-monoidal structure and transport it to \(f^*\) along the
2666	  uniqueness of left adjoints. This mirrors Mathlib's own construction of the
2667	  extension-of-scalars monoidal structure in
2668	  \texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Monoidal/Adjunction.lean}, where the
2669	  \emph{left} adjoint's strong-monoidal structure is built explicitly (via the
2670	  base-change distributivity isomorphism) and only afterwards is the right adjoint's
2671	  lax structure derived from the adjunction --- never the reverse direction. The
2672	  argument has three steps.
2673	
2674	  \emph{Step 1 (a concrete strong-monoidal pullback \(P\)).} Build the concrete functor
2675	  \[
2676	    P \;=\; \mathtt{sheafification} \;\circ\; (\text{presheaf base change}),
2677	  \]
2678	  the presheaf-level inverse image (sectionwise extension of scalars along the
2679	  structure-sheaf ring maps of \(f\)) followed by sheafification on the small Zariski
2680	  site --- the same \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\) shape already used in
2681	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. Equip \(P\) with a strong-monoidal structure. Its
2682	  tensorator is the linear-algebra strong-monoidal core
2683	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.distribBaseChange}\),
2684	  \[
2685	    (M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S
2686	      \;\cong\;
2687	    (M \otimes_R S) \otimes_S (N \otimes_R S),
2688	  \]
2689	  an isomorphism for \emph{every} ring map \(R \to S\), with no flatness, sheafified
2690	  pointwise; this is precisely Mathlib's \((\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\varphi).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
2691	  lifted to sheaves. The unit comparison is the already-landed sheafification-monoidality
2692	  brick \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\). Thus \(P\) is strong monoidal with each
2693	  tensorator and the unit comparison an isomorphism, with no hypothesis on \(f\).
2694	
2695	  \emph{Step 2 (\(P\) is left adjoint to pushforward).} Exhibit an adjunction
2696	  \(P \dashv \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,f\). This is the universal property
2697	  of sheafified extension of scalars: the presheaf base change is left adjoint to
2698	  presheaf restriction of scalars, and sheafification is left adjoint to the inclusion,
2699	  so the composite \(P\) is left adjoint to the pushforward.
2700	
2701	  \emph{Step 3 (transport to the abstract pullback).} By definition
2702	  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f = (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\)

2937	\section{The invertibility-carrier Picard group}
2938	\label{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}
2939	
2940	The group law of \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} is carried on the
2941	\emph{locally-trivial} predicate \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), and on that carrier
2942	the existence of a tensor inverse for each class is an extra obligation
2943	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}): one must \emph{manufacture} the dual
2944	object \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) and prove the
2945	contraction \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), which is the whole content of
2946	the dual-infrastructure build of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
2947	
2948	This section carries the group law on a \emph{different} predicate for which that
2949	obligation evaporates: the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2950	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
2951	\[
2952	  \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
2953	  \;:\equiv\;
2954	  \exists\, N,\ \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\bigr).
2955	\]
2956	On this carrier the inverse of a class \([M]\) is supplied by the \emph{membership
2957	witness itself} --- the very \(N\) whose existence \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
2958	asserts --- so no inverse object need be constructed and no separate
2959	tensor-inverse lemma is consumed. The cost is shifted to a routine
2960	well-definedness fact (\cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef}: the witness \(N\) is
2961	determined up to isomorphism), proved from the monoidal coherence isomorphisms
2962	alone. The carrier is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
2963	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), whose elements are exactly the iso-classes of invertible
2964	modules (Stacks tag 01CX).

4066	\begin{lemma}
4067	\label{lemma-invertible}
4068	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Let $\mathcal{L}$
4069	be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Equivalent are
4070	\begin{enumerate}
4071	\item $\mathcal{L}$ is invertible, and
4072	\item there exists an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{N}$
4073	such that
4074	$\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X$.
4075	\end{enumerate}
4076	In this case $\mathcal{L}$ is locally a direct summand of a finite free
4077	$\mathcal{O}_X$-module and the module $\mathcal{N}$ in (2) is isomorphic to
4078	$\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$.
4079	\end{lemma}
4080	
4081	\begin{proof}
4082	Assume (1). Then the functor $- \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
4083	is essentially surjective, hence there exists an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
4084	$\mathcal{N}$ as in (2). If (2) holds, then the functor
4085	$- \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N}$ is a quasi-inverse
4086	to the functor $- \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$ and
4087	we see that (1) holds.
4088	
4089	\medskip\noindent
4090	Assume (1) and (2) hold. Denote
4091	$\psi : \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \to \mathcal{O}_X$
4092	the given isomorphism. Let $x \in X$. Choose an open neighbourhood
4093	$U$ an integer $n \geq 1$ and sections $s_i \in \mathcal{L}(U)$,
4094	$t_i \in \mathcal{N}(U)$ such that $\psi(\sum s_i \otimes t_i) = 1$.
4095	Consider the isomorphisms
4096	$$
4097	\mathcal{L}|_U \to
4098	\mathcal{L}|_U \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_U}
4099	\mathcal{L}|_U \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_U} \mathcal{N}|_U \to \mathcal{L}|_U
4100	$$
4101	where the first arrow sends $s$ to $\sum s_i \otimes s \otimes t_i$
4102	and the second arrow sends $s \otimes s' \otimes t$ to $\psi(s' \otimes t)s$.
4103	We conclude that $s \mapsto \sum \psi(s \otimes t_i)s_i$ is
4104	an automorphism of $\mathcal{L}|_U$. This automorphism factors as
4105	$$
4106	\mathcal{L}|_U \to \mathcal{O}_U^{\oplus n} \to \mathcal{L}|_U
4107	$$
4108	where the first arrow is given by
4109	$s \mapsto (\psi(s \otimes t_1), \ldots, \psi(s \otimes t_n))$
4110	and the second arrow by $(a_1, \ldots, a_n) \mapsto \sum a_i s_i$.
4111	In this way we conclude that $\mathcal{L}|_U$ is a direct summand
4112	of a finite free $\mathcal{O}_U$-module.
4113	
4114	\medskip\noindent
4115	Assume (1) and (2) hold. Consider the evaluation map
4116	$$
4117	\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
4118	\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)
4119	\longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_X
4120	$$
4121	To finish the proof of the lemma
4122	we will show this is an isomorphism by checking it induces
4123	isomorphisms on stalks. Let $x \in X$.
4124	Since we know (by the previous paragraph)
4125	that $\mathcal{L}$ is a finitely presented
4126	$\mathcal{O}_X$-module
4127	we can use Lemma \ref{lemma-stalk-internal-hom}
4128	to see that it suffices to show that
4129	$$
4130	\mathcal{L}_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}}
4131	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}}(\mathcal{L}_x, \mathcal{O}_{X, x})
4132	\longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{X, x}
4133	$$
4134	is an isomorphism. Since
4135	$\mathcal{L}_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}} \mathcal{N}_x =
4136	(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N})_x =
4137	\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ (Lemma \ref{lemma-stalk-tensor-product})
4138	the desired result follows from
4139	More on Algebra, Lemma \ref{more-algebra-lemma-invertible}.

4142	\begin{lemma}
4143	\label{lemma-pullback-invertible}
4144	Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a
4145	morphism of ringed spaces. The pullback $f^*\mathcal{L}$ of an
4146	invertible $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module is invertible.
4147	\end{lemma}
4148	
4149	\begin{proof}
4150	By Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}
4151	there exists an $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module $\mathcal{N}$ such that
4152	$\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_Y} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_Y$.
4153	Pulling back we get
4154	$f^*\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} f^*\mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X$
4155	by Lemma \ref{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}.
4156	Thus $f^*\mathcal{L}$ is invertible by Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}.

4159	\begin{lemma}
4160	\label{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}
4161	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
4162	$\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
4163	If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
4164	the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case).
4165	\end{lemma}
4166	
4167	\begin{proof}
4168	The parenthetical statement follows by considering a one point
4169	space $X$ with sheaf of rings $\mathcal{O}_X$ given by a ring $R$.
4170	Then invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules correspond to invertible
4171	$R$-modules, hence as soon as $\Pic(R)$ is not the trivial group,
4172	then we get an example.
4173	
4174	\medskip\noindent
4175	Assume $\mathcal{L}$ is locally free of rank $1$ and consider the
4176	evaluation map
4177	$$
4178	\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
4179	\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)
4180	\longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_X
4181	$$
4182	Looking over an open covering trivialization $\mathcal{L}$, we see
4183	that this map is an isomorphism. Hence $\mathcal{L}$ is invertible
4184	by Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}.
4185	
4186	\medskip\noindent
4187	Assume all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings and $\mathcal{L}$
4188	invertible. In the proof of Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}

The relative Picard consumer (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) re-bases its
line-bundle carrier onto the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and its
functorial structure maps are \emph{pullback} maps of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules: the
projection pullback \(\pi_T^*\) along \(C \times_S T \to T\) and the base-change
pullback along \(C \times_S T' \to C \times_S T\). For these maps to descend to the
\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) carrier one needs that \emph{pullback preserves
\(\otimes\)-invertibility} for \emph{general} morphisms of schemes --- the
projection and the base-change morphism are neither open immersions nor flat.

This is a genuinely different fact from the open-immersion compatibility
\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and not a corollary of it. That lemma handles
restriction along an open immersion, and it does so by routing through the
\emph{lax right} adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is
strong monoidal there \emph{only} because an open immersion's structure-sheaf ring
map is a local \emph{isomorphism} (\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\)). A general scheme
morphism has no such isomorphism, so the right-adjoint route is unavailable.

The general case is the \emph{left} adjoint, pullback \(f^* = \) inverse image
\(=(\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\). Conceptually pullback \emph{is}
``extension of scalars'', and extension of scalars along any ring map \emph{is} strong
monoidal --- the base-change tensorator \((M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S \cong
(M \otimes_R S) \otimes_S (N \otimes_R S)\) is an isomorphism with no flatness
hypothesis. So at the level of mathematics there is no obstacle: pullback commutes
with \(\otimes\) for every morphism. The one genuine subtlety is purely structural to
the formalisation: the project carries \(f^*\) \emph{abstractly}, as the left adjoint
\((\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\), so that \((f^*).\mathrm{obj}\) has
no sectionwise value to which one could literally attach the \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)
tensorator. The resolution is the standard one for an abstract left adjoint: build the
strong-monoidal structure on a \emph{concrete} model \(P\) of the pullback and transport
it to \(f^*\) along the uniqueness of left adjoints. This dissolves the
``no sectionwise formula'' worry entirely --- one never computes \(f^*\) sectionwise;
one transports a concrete monoidal structure across an adjoint-uniqueness isomorphism.
This is exactly the device that closed \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.

It is worth recording why two tempting shortcuts are not taken. First, one might hope
that since pullback is \emph{some} (op)lax monoidal functor, invertible objects are
preserved for free. This is \emph{false}: global sections \(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal,
yet \(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not an invertible module, so a mere
oplax comparison map does \emph{not} suffice --- one genuinely has to prove the tensor
comparison is an \emph{isomorphism}. Second, one might route through local triviality,
``invertible \(\Rightarrow\) locally \(\mathcal{O}\) \(\Rightarrow\) pulls back to
locally \(\mathcal{O}\)''. This is a mirage on the present carrier: recovering
\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) from local triviality is the reverse bridge
\(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsInvertible}\), i.e.\ exactly the
dual/tensor-inverse manufacture that the invertibility-carrier pivot
(\cref{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}) was built to delete. Restricting to \emph{flat}
structure maps does not help either: flatness governs the exactness of \(f^*\), not
whether the tensor comparison is an isomorphism (it is an isomorphism for \emph{every}
morphism, flat or not). The strong-monoidal pullback route is therefore primary
precisely because it pulls back the existing tensor-inverse witness directly, through a
comparison isomorphism valid for all morphisms, never reintroducing the deleted dual.

The relative Picard consumer (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) re-bases its
line-bundle carrier onto the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and its
functorial structure maps are \emph{pullback} maps of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules: the
projection pullback \(\pi_T^*\) along \(C \times_S T \to T\) and the base-change
pullback along \(C \times_S T' \to C \times_S T\). For these maps to descend to the
\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) carrier one needs that \emph{pullback preserves
\(\otimes\)-invertibility} for \emph{general} morphisms of schemes --- the
projection and the base-change morphism are neither open immersions nor flat. This
is the target lemma \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} of the section.

This is a genuinely different fact from the open-immersion compatibility
\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and not a corollary of it. That lemma handles
restriction along an open immersion, and it does so by routing through the
\emph{lax right} adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is
strong monoidal there \emph{only} because an open immersion's structure-sheaf ring
map is a local \emph{isomorphism} (\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\)). A general scheme
morphism has no such isomorphism, so the right-adjoint route is unavailable.

\paragraph{The general comparison is descoped.} One might attempt
\cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} through the \emph{general} pullback--tensor
comparison \(f^*(M \otimes_X N) \cong f^*M \otimes_Y f^*N\) for \emph{arbitrary}
\(M, N\) (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}). Mathematically that isomorphism is true
for every morphism --- pullback is extension of scalars and extension of scalars is
strong monoidal --- but \emph{formalising the general iso is Mathlib-scale and is not
needed on any project path}. The obstruction is structural: the project carries
\(f^*\) \emph{abstractly}, as the left adjoint
\((\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\), with no sectionwise value to which
one could attach the extension-of-scalars tensorator. Building the comparison as an
isomorphism by the concrete-model route would require a concrete model \(P =
\mathtt{sheafification} \circ (\text{presheaf base change})\), and that model needs
two ingredients absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit: a presheaf-level
extension-of-scalars functor \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.extendScalars}\), and a
topological inverse-image functor (a left Kan extension along
\((\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}).\mathrm{op}\)). The Stacks proof of the
general statement is itself recorded as ``Omitted''
(\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}): it is standard mathematics with no slick
formalisation. We therefore \emph{descope} the general iso and prove only the
invertible case, which is all the downstream consumers (relative Picard functor,
Quot embedding, tangent computation, Albanese universal property) ever require.

\paragraph{The route: local trivialisation of the invertible pair.} For invertible
\(M, N\) the comparison map \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}} : f^*(M \otimes_X N) \to f^*M
\otimes_Y f^*N\) (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}) \emph{is} an isomorphism, and we
prove this not in general but on the specific invertible pair, by local
trivialisation. The comparison map itself exists for arbitrary \(M, N\): it is the
sheaf-level transport of the presheaf-level oplax comparison
(\cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}) through sheafification, and it sidesteps
the absent inverse image because it is only a \emph{map}, not an iso. Invertibility
enters only to upgrade that map to an iso: an invertible module is locally free of
rank \(1\) (\cref{lem:isinvertible_implies_locallytrivial}), so \(M, N\), and hence
\(M \otimes_X N\), become \(\mathcal{O}_X\) on a common affine cover; their pullbacks
become \(\mathcal{O}_Y\) on the preimage cover (\(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}\)),
where \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) restricts to the canonical comparison
\(\mathcal{O} \otimes \mathcal{O} \cong \mathcal{O}\), an isomorphism; and a morphism
of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules that is an isomorphism after restriction to every member
of a cover is an isomorphism (\cref{lem:isIso_of_isIso_restrict}).

\paragraph{Two negative results that pin the route.} First, one might hope that since
pullback is \emph{some} (op)lax monoidal functor, invertible objects are preserved for
free. This is \emph{false}: global sections \(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal, yet
\(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not an invertible module, so a mere
oplax comparison map does \emph{not} suffice --- one genuinely has to prove the tensor
comparison is an \emph{isomorphism}. This is exactly why
\cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} cannot be read off the presheaf-level oplax structure
of \cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal} alone, and must localise.

Second --- and crucially --- the local-trivialisation route is \emph{not} the shelved
``invertible \(\Rightarrow\) locally \(\mathcal{O}\) \(\Rightarrow\) recover
invertibility from local triviality'' mirage. That mirage is the \emph{reverse} bridge
\(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsInvertible}\), the dual/tensor-inverse
manufacture that the invertibility-carrier pivot (\cref{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}) was
built to delete; it stays off-path here. Our route never recovers invertibility from
local triviality: it produces the explicit witness \(f^*N\) directly and uses local
triviality \emph{only} in the \emph{forward} direction
\(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)
(\cref{lem:isinvertible_implies_locallytrivial}, invertible \(\Rightarrow\) locally free
of rank \(1\), which is genuinely available), and only to certify that the comparison
map \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) is an isomorphism on that one pair.

\paragraph{Guardrail.} The iso-ness of \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) must be checked
\emph{on a cover} via \cref{lem:isIso_of_isIso_restrict}, \emph{not} stalkwise.
Reducing it to stalks would re-derive the varying-ring stalk-tensor commutation that
sits behind the abandoned ``d.2'' stalk-tensor sink; the cover route avoids that
entirely, because on each trivialising open all three modules are literally
\(\mathcal{O}\) and the comparison is the canonical \(\mathcal{O} \otimes \mathcal{O}
\cong \mathcal{O}\).

\begin{lemma}
[The presheaf pushforward is lax monoidal]
  \label{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.presheafPushforwardLaxMonoidal}
  Let \(\varphi\) be a morphism of \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued ring presheaves,
  presenting a pushforward of presheaves of modules. Then the presheaf pushforward
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\) is \emph{lax monoidal}: it
  carries the comparison
  \[
    \mu :
      (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
        \otimes (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
      \;\longrightarrow\;
      (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,(A \otimes B),
  \]
  obtained as the composite of the strong-monoidal pushforward of presheaves over a
  fixed ring presheaf (\(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\)) with the lax
  monoidal restriction of scalars \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) along the
  ring-presheaf map. This is a statement at the \emph{presheaf} level of
  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules; it does not require a monoidal structure on
  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  Restriction of scalars along a homomorphism of ring presheaves is lax monoidal --- the
  comparison sends \((a \otimes b)\) to itself under the identification of the
  restricted tensor product with the source tensor product --- and the pushforward of
  presheaves of modules over a \emph{fixed} ring presheaf is strong monoidal. The
  presheaf pushforward along \(\varphi\) factors as the composite of these two functors,
  so its lax monoidal structure is the composite of the strong-monoidal structure on the
  first factor with the lax structure on the second; the comparison \(\mu\) is the
  whiskered composite of the two comparisons.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[The presheaf pullback is oplax monoidal; the comparison map $\delta$]
  \label{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.presheafPullbackOplaxMonoidal}
  \uses{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}
  In the same setting, the presheaf pullback \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\)
  --- the left adjoint of the lax-monoidal presheaf pushforward of
  \cref{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal} --- is \emph{oplax monoidal}. In
  particular it carries the canonical comparison \emph{morphism}
  \[
    \delta :
      (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,(A \otimes B)
      \;\longrightarrow\;
      (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
        \otimes (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B,
  \]
  produced by the doctrinal adjunction transfer
  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\): the oplax structure on a left
  adjoint is the adjunction mate of the lax structure on its right adjoint. The
  comparison \(\delta\) is at the \emph{presheaf} level, and it is only a
  \emph{morphism}: upgrading \(\delta\) to an isomorphism is the remaining content, and
  is exactly what the local-trivialisation argument of \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback}
  supplies for an invertible pair.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}
  The presheaf pullback is by construction the left adjoint of the presheaf pushforward,
  which is lax monoidal by \cref{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}. Mathlib's
  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\) turns the lax-monoidal structure on a
  right adjoint into an oplax-monoidal structure on the left adjoint, with the oplax
  comparison \(\delta\) defined as the mate of the lax comparison \(\mu\) under the
  adjunction. Applying this to the pullback--pushforward adjunction yields the asserted
  oplax structure and the comparison morphism \(\delta\).
\end{proof}

The locus of the difficulty is precise. A presheaf-level oplax comparison
  \emph{map} \(\delta\) already exists and costs nothing: the presheaf pushforward is lax
  monoidal (\cref{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}) and its left adjoint, the
  presheaf pullback, is therefore oplax monoidal
  (\cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}), via the bundled doctrinal adjunction
  transfer \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\). (This corrects an earlier
  belief that there was ``no \(\mathtt{pushforward.LaxMonoidal}\)''; there is one, at the
  presheaf level.) The comparison \emph{map} \(\delta\) is thus free; the entire residual
  difficulty is upgrading \(\delta\) to an \emph{isomorphism} for arbitrary \(M, N\).
  This is what Step~3's transport along \(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\) would deliver --- but
  only once the concrete model \(P\) of Step~1 is constructed, and \(P\) requires the
  Mathlib-absent presheaf extension of scalars and topological inverse image. There is no
  shortcut from the bare oplax map: an (op)lax monoidal functor does \emph{not} preserve
  invertible objects for free --- global sections \(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal yet
  \(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not invertible --- so the iso must be
  proved, not inferred from the doctrinal structure.

  Because only the invertible case is needed, the substrate does not build \(P\) and does
  not prove the general iso. Instead it works with the comparison map \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\)
  of \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map} (the sheaf-level transport of the presheaf \(\delta\))
  and proves it is an isomorphism on a single invertible pair by local trivialisation
  (\cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback}), which never invokes \(P\) or the absent inverse
  image.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[The sheaf-level pullback--tensor comparison map $\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}$]
  \label{lem:pullback_tensor_map}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackTensorMap}
  \uses{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
  There is a canonical comparison \emph{morphism}
  \[
    \delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}} :
      (\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,(M \otimes_X N)
      \;\longrightarrow\;
      (\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,M
        \otimes_Y (\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,N
  \]
  of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules, natural in \(M\) and \(N\). It is a \emph{map only}: in
  general it is not asserted to be an isomorphism.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
  The presheaf pullback is oplax monoidal (\cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}),
  so at the presheaf level it carries the comparison morphism
  \[
    \delta : (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,(A \otimes B)
      \;\longrightarrow\;
      (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
        \otimes (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B,
  \]
  where \(\varphi\) is the ring-presheaf map presenting \(f\). One transports \(\delta\)
  through sheafification to the sheaf level, using the same
  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\) device that supplies Steps 1--2 of
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}: the abstract pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
  agrees, up to canonical natural isomorphism, with the sheafification of the presheaf
  pullback, and likewise the sheafified tensor product \(\otimes_Y\) is the
  sheafification of the presheaf tensor. The right-hand side reconciliation of the unit
  and tensor factors with their sheafified forms is supplied by the
  sheafification-monoidality brick \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\). Whiskering and
  composing these canonical isomorphisms with the sheafification of \(\delta\) produces
  the asserted sheaf-level morphism \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\), natural in \(M, N\)
  because each ingredient is. No claim of invertibility is made: \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\)
  is the carrier on which the invertible-case isomorphism argument of
  \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} acts.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[An invertible module is locally trivial (locally free of rank $1$)]
  \label{lem:isinvertible_implies_locallytrivial}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible.isLocallyTrivial}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, def:line_bundle_islocally_trivial}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). If \(M\) is
  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), then \(M\) is locally
  trivial: every point of \(X\) has an open (affine) neighbourhood \(U\) on which
  \(M|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). In symbols
  \[
    \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
      \;\Longrightarrow\;
    \mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,M.
  \]
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
  % \ref{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1} (invertible modules and locally free
  % rank-1 modules coincide when all stalks are local rings)
  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4159-4165).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{lemma}
  % \label{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}
  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
  % If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
  % the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case).
  % \end{lemma}"
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
  % \ref{lemma-invertible} (invertibility characterised by a tensor inverse; an
  % invertible module is locally a direct summand of a finite free module)
  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4066-4079).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{lemma}
  % \label{lemma-invertible}
  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Let $\mathcal{L}$
  % be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Equivalent are
  % \begin{enumerate}
  % \item $\mathcal{L}$ is invertible, and
  % \item there exists an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{N}$
  % such that
  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X$.
  % \end{enumerate}
  % In this case $\mathcal{L}$ is locally a direct summand of a finite free
  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-module and the module $\mathcal{N}$ in (2) is isomorphic to
  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$.
  % \end{lemma}"
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemmas
  \texttt{lemma-invertible} and \texttt{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}: on a
  ringed space whose stalks are local rings, an invertible module is locally free of
  rank \(1\); and an invertible module is finitely presented (locally a direct summand of
  a finite free module).}
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4186-4198).
% "Assume all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings and $\mathcal{L}$
% invertible. In the proof of Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}
% we have seen that $\mathcal{L}_x$ is an invertible
% $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$-module for all $x \in X$. Since
% $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ is local, we see that
% $\mathcal{L}_x \cong \mathcal{O}_{X, x}$
% (More on Algebra, Section \ref{more-algebra-section-picard}).
% Since $\mathcal{L}$ is of finite presentation by
% Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible} we conclude that $\mathcal{L}$
% is locally free of rank $1$ by
% Lemma \ref{lemma-finite-presentation-stalk-free}."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Lemma \texttt{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}
  (converse direction), using \texttt{lemma-invertible}.}
  This is the route's one genuinely new cost; the stalk-tensor ingredient it depends on
  is already in the tree.

  By \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) there is a
  witness \(N\) and an isomorphism \(\psi : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim}
  \mathcal{O}_X\); by Stacks \texttt{lemma-invertible} this already forces \(M\) to be
  finitely presented (locally a direct summand of a finite free \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module).
  Fix \(x \in X\). Taking stalks of \(\psi\) and commuting the stalk past the tensor
  product --- the varying-ring stalk-tensor identification
  \((M \otimes_X N)_x \cong M_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X,x}} N_x\) (the d.2 commutation
  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\), already proven) --- gives
  \[
    M_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X,x}} N_x \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_{X,x},
  \]
  so \(M_x\) is an invertible module over the \emph{local} ring \(\mathcal{O}_{X,x}\)
  (the scheme hypothesis on \(X\) supplies local stalks). An invertible module over a
  local ring is free of rank \(1\), so \(M_x \cong \mathcal{O}_{X,x}\). Since \(M\) is
  finitely presented and its stalk at \(x\) is free, the standard finite-presentation
  spreading-out (Stacks \texttt{lemma-finite-presentation-stalk-free}) extends the free
  stalk to a free neighbourhood: there is an open \(U \ni x\) with \(M|_U \cong
  \mathcal{O}_U\). As \(x\) was arbitrary and any such \(U\) may be shrunk to an affine
  open, \(M\) is locally trivial.
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, lem:pullback_tensor_map,
        lem:isinvertible_implies_locallytrivial, lem:pullback_unit_iso,
        lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Lemma \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}.}
  This realises the Stacks proof in the project's notation, performing the
  \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} step (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}) only on
  the invertible pair --- never as the general isomorphism --- by local trivialisation.

  By \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) there is a
  witness \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
  \(e : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\). We take the pullback witness
  \((\mathtt{pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,N = f^*N\), and exhibit \(f^*M\) as
  \(\otimes\)-invertible by the composite
  \[
    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)
      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}^{-1}\;}\;
    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;f^*e\;}\;
    f^*\mathcal{O}_X
      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;\mathtt{pullbackUnitIso}\;}\;
    \mathcal{O}_Y,
  \]
  where \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}} = \mathtt{pullbackTensorMap}\,M\,N\) is the comparison
  map of \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map} and \(\mathtt{pullbackUnitIso}\) identifies
  \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X\) with \(\mathcal{O}_Y\) (\cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}). The middle
  and last arrows are isomorphisms outright; the only content is that
  \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) is an \emph{isomorphism} for this invertible pair.

  \emph{$\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}$ is an isomorphism on the invertible pair.} Being
  invertible, \(M\) and \(N\) are locally trivial
  (\cref{lem:isinvertible_implies_locallytrivial}); choose a common affine cover
  \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which \(M|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and \(N|_{U_i}
  \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). On each \(U_i\) the tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) is also trivial
  (\(\cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) by \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}). The
  preimages \(\{f^{-1}U_i\}\) form an open cover of \(Y\). By
  \cref{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict} it suffices to show that the restriction of
  \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) to each \(f^{-1}U_i\) is an isomorphism.

  Fix \(i\). On \(f^{-1}U_i\) the modules \(f^*M\), \(f^*N\), and \(f^*(M \otimes_X N)\)
  all restrict to \(\mathcal{O}_{f^{-1}U_i}\): the pullback of a locally-trivial module is
  locally trivial (\cref{lem:IsLocallyTrivial_pullback}), and over \(U_i\) the three
  source modules are already \(\mathcal{O}\), so by naturality of restriction with pullback
  their restrictions to \(f^{-1}U_i\) are \(\mathcal{O}_{f^{-1}U_i}\). Under these
  trivialisations the restriction of \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) is the canonical comparison
  \(\mathcal{O} \otimes_{f^{-1}U_i} \mathcal{O} \to \mathcal{O}\), which is an isomorphism
  (its inverse is the unit coherence \(\mathcal{O} \cong \mathcal{O} \otimes \mathcal{O}\)).
  The compatibility ``\(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) restricts to the canonical comparison''
  --- i.e.\ that the local trivialisations intertwine \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}|_{f^{-1}U_i}\)
  with \(\mathcal{O} \otimes \mathcal{O} \cong \mathcal{O}\) --- is the crux to discharge;
  it holds because \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) is natural in \(M, N\) and the comparison of
  the unit with itself is the identity. The iso-ness is checked on the cover, \emph{never}
  stalkwise. Hence \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) is an isomorphism, the composite above is
  defined, and \(f^*N\) is a tensor inverse of \(f^*M\), so \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(f^*M)\)
  holds.
\end{proof}
